name: Stagehand
maintenance_status: Active. The TypeScript monorepo was pushed on 2026-07-09 and npm reported @browserbasehq/stagehand 3.6.0; the Python repository released 3.21.0 on 2026-05-29 and was pushed on 2026-06-09. Stagehand v3 documentation is current and covers local/Browserbase execution, Python, caching, self-healing, structured observe/act/extract operations, and agent execution.
license: MIT for the TypeScript and Python SDKs, compatible with ApplyPilot's AGPL-3.0 codebase when notices are retained. Browserbase cloud, Model Gateway, proxies, recordings, and managed caches are commercial services governed separately; the open-source license does not grant those services or override site terms.
pricing_model: Open-source local Stagehand has no framework fee and uses a selected model plus local compute. Browserbase plans currently include Free at $0 with 1 browser hour and 3 agent runs, Developer at $20/month with 100 browser hours then $0.12/hour, and Startup at $99/month with 500 browser hours then $0.10/hour. Model Gateway is pay-as-you-go at upstream market token prices, and proxy overage is separately metered.
marginal_cost_per_attempt: [uncertain] Planning scenario for a five-minute Browserbase attempt: browser overage is $0.010 at $0.12/hour. At 30,000 input and 3,000 output tokens, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite costs about $0.0042 and Gemini 2.5 Flash about $0.0165 at current primary-source rates, producing an estimated $0.0142-$0.0265 attempt before proxy, human work, and fallback. Included browser hours can make immediate cash marginal cost zero until exhausted, but opportunity cost remains.
projected_cost_per_verified_apply: [uncertain] Using ApplyPilot historical success rates and the $0.0142-$0.0265 planning attempt cost with no fallback gives lower bounds of about $0.04-$0.08 for Ashby at 33.7%, $0.05-$0.09 for Greenhouse at 28.6%, $0.17-$0.32 for other hosts at 8.3%, and $1.42-$2.65 for Workday at 1.0%. Add fallback_rate multiplied by the measured $0.8599 current Claude attempt cost to the numerator, plus human recovery, before dividing by verified completion; therefore even modest fallback can erase the apparent savings.
billing_granularity: Browserbase includes monthly browser-hour allowances, then meters browser hours; current plan pages do not state a smaller production unit than browser time. Model Gateway passes through market token prices without markup. Developer proxy overage is $12/GB and Startup overage is $10/GB. Idle keep-alive sessions consume browser time, and cache hits remove Stagehand model inference but not browser or proxy cost.
model_cost: [uncertain] Stagehand supports many providers, local Ollama, custom endpoints, and Browserbase Model Gateway. Current Gemini rates used for planning are $0.10/$0.40 per million input/output tokens for 2.5 Flash-Lite and $0.30/$2.50 for 2.5 Flash. Actual Stagehand token volume on ATS forms is unmeasured; server and local caches can make repeated act/observe/agent operations use $0 model inference on a hit.
infrastructure_cost: [uncertain] Local mode needs Chrome/Chromium, Node for the TypeScript SDK or the Python package's embedded/local service, CPU/RAM, cache storage, and model connectivity. Browserbase moves browser CPU/RAM, recording, and network capture into its browser-hour price. Five-minute planning cost is $0.010 at Developer overage or about $0.0083 at Startup overage, excluding included hours and proxy bytes.
human_recovery_cost: [uncertain] Browserbase live session visibility and persistent contexts can support takeover, but Stagehand has no ApplyPilot-specific OTP or owner-queue workflow. At $50/hour, five operator minutes cost $4.17; expected labor cost is $4.17 times the intervention rate. A 5% recovery rate adds about $0.21 per attempt, far more than the planning model/browser cost.
adapter_maintenance_tco: [uncertain] Planning estimate: 2-4 engineer-weeks for Python/TypeScript integration, answer-plan enforcement, cache key/version policy, confirmation extraction, observability joins, and ATS canaries, followed by 8-16 engineering hours per month for Stagehand/API/model and site drift. Amortize loaded hours over verified applications and compare against deterministic adapter maintenance, because low token prices do not eliminate semantic workflow repair.
verified_completion_rate: [uncertain] No primary source reports positive, ATS-stratified job-application completion with exactly-once safeguards. Stagehand product examples and general browser-agent evaluations cannot substitute for ApplyPilot's Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday, and long-tail benchmark. Assign 0% production credit until a canary records durable confirmation evidence.
failure_modes: Expected failures include stale or unstable accessibility trees causing cache misses, self-heal choosing the wrong element, conditional fields appearing after an action, model schema errors, unsupported file or rich-text widgets, browser or CDP loss, Stagehand API/model rate limits, Browserbase session timeout, profile/context mismatch, cache replay after a meaningful DOM change, login/OTP/challenge walls, and an agent claiming success without durable submit evidence.
crash_recovery: keepAlive can leave local or Browserbase sessions running and Browserbase sessions can be reattached by ID. Local action/agent caches can replay known actions, while structured logs, CDP events, and Browserbase recordings support diagnosis. There is no job-level transactional resume; after any submit-touching action, ApplyPilot must inspect evidence and quarantine ambiguity rather than replaying the cached sequence.
exactly_once_submit_safety: Stagehand does not supply application deduplication, a submission ledger, or an atomic submit/confirm transaction. observe can preview an action before act, which helps planning but is not an idempotency guarantee. Keep final submit in a narrow ApplyPilot function with lease validation, plan hash, one-time token, network/DOM verification, and crash_unconfirmed quarantine.
verification_evidence_tier: Stagehand can extract typed confirmation data, use a Playwright/custom page, expose CDP-based page responses, and rely on Browserbase network monitoring, console logs, screenshots, and session replay. It reaches a high evidence tier only when an ApplyPilot verifier records an ATS/application ID or explicit successful response; model output or an extracted success message alone is lower-confidence and a recording is supporting evidence, not proof.
verification_latency: [uncertain] DOM extraction and Browserbase network/session evidence are available synchronously, so normal confirmation should be seconds after submit. Delayed email evidence remains outside Stagehand. The additional API/model round trips and DOM-settle delay have not been measured on ApplyPilot ATS pages.
headless_headed_parity: [uncertain] Local Stagehand supports headless and visible Chrome, while Browserbase provides remote browsers with live view and recording. No official ATS-specific parity data exists. Benchmark login, file upload, dynamic widgets, challenges, and confirmation in local headed, local headless, and Browserbase modes before sharing route policy.
python_integration: Good and current. The official stagehand Python package requires Python 3.9+ and supports synchronous and asynchronous clients, local mode, remote sessions, and Bring Your Own Browser patterns with Playwright or other drivers. ApplyPilot can remain Python-first, although the TypeScript implementation has the broadest direct local-library surface and Python v3 API semantics should be pinned and tested.
playwright_cdp_reuse: [uncertain] Stagehand explicitly accepts Playwright Page objects, exposes a CDP connection URL, supports a fixed local CDP port, and the Python SDK uses BYOB patterns. Reusing ApplyPilot's already-running seeded Chrome tab rather than a Stagehand-created or Browserbase session appears feasible through BYOB/CDP, but ownership, profile locking, and full feature parity need a prototype before this is treated as supported production behavior.
migration_effort: [uncertain] Medium. A shadow-mode integration can call observe/extract around the current Playwright page with limited change; production submit requires route selection, cache storage/versioning, Python SDK lifecycle, model configuration, answer provenance, evidence capture, and fail-closed final action. Keep existing deterministic Greenhouse logic as the control instead of replacing it.
ats_fit: [uncertain] Promising for variable long-tail forms and for semantic discovery on stable ATS variants, especially when observed actions can be cached. Deterministic adapters remain preferable for Ashby and Greenhouse. Workday's 1.0% historical success and tenant authentication make it a poor unattended target regardless of Stagehand flexibility; Lever, Workable, and long-tail hosts need separate canaries.
deterministic_coverage: [uncertain] Cache misses are model-assisted, but local cache can replay saved act and agent actions with no LLM call, and Browserbase automatically caches act/observe/extract requests. The effective no-model share is the valid warm-cache hit rate after excluding stale or unsafe replays; no ApplyPilot hit rate exists.
escape_rate: [uncertain] Unknown. Measure the share of eligible jobs that fail observation, produce an incomplete required-field plan, miss cache, exceed budget, encounter auth/challenge, or require a premium agent/human. A cache miss is not necessarily an escape if the configured cheap model can safely repair the action; a post-submit uncertainty is quarantine, not fallback.
control_plane_overhead: [uncertain] Each uncached observe, act, extract, or agent step serializes page/accessibility context and makes an API/model round trip; DOM settling adds latency. Selector scoping, variables, batched observed actions, local/server cache, and direct Playwright code can reduce context. Instrument input/output tokens, action count, page snapshot bytes, cache status, and wall time per step.
observability_joinability: Strong. Stagehand can write structured Stagehand, LLM, and CDP event logs per session; Browserbase adds session IDs, recordings, network requests, console logs, CPU/RAM, duration, proxy bytes, and cost data. Store attempt_id in session metadata and persist session ID, cache status, model, token usage, action/result hashes, recording URL or local artifact paths, and final evidence in apply_result_events.
persistent_profiles: Local launch options support userDataDir and preservation, while Browserbase offers persistent contexts and existing session IDs. keepAlive can retain browser state after process close. ApplyPilot needs one account/profile owner, exclusive locks, explicit context persistence on clean shutdown, encrypted storage, and canaries across Chrome upgrades.
existing_session_attach: [uncertain] Reattaching an existing Browserbase session is first-class, and Stagehand can operate on custom Playwright/Puppeteer/Patchright pages. A local fixed CDP port and BYOB architecture provide the pieces for attaching to an ApplyPilot Chrome session, but current documentation emphasizes Stagehand-created local browsers or Browserbase sessions. Verify safe attachment and disconnect behavior on Windows before relying on it.
login_and_otp: Persistent contexts, supplied cookies, variables that keep secret values out of prompts, Browserbase live visibility, and keepAlive support owner-assisted login. There is no built-in ApplyPilot inbox polling, OTP matching, or single-use code ledger. Route login and OTP to the existing owner/home recovery lane and resume only after deterministic authentication checks.
extension_support: [uncertain] Local Chrome launch options accept arbitrary args, so a reviewed unpacked extension may be loadable, and BYOB can use a browser page created elsewhere. Stagehand does not document a first-class narrowly permissioned extension lifecycle comparable to Chrome DevTools MCP, and managed Browserbase extension constraints may differ. Prototype before selecting this architecture.
session_data_boundary: With local library mode and Stagehand API disabled, browser execution stays local, but page context still goes to the configured model unless it is local. Browserbase mode sends cookies, page data, resumes/uploads, network traffic, recordings, and managed cache inputs to Browserbase and model inputs to Model Gateway/provider. Current Browserbase pricing states 7-day retention for Free and Developer, 30 days for Startup, and 30+ days for Scale; local cache/log files may also contain sensitive action data and must be protected.
required_field_completeness: observe can discover structured candidate actions and extract can validate typed data, but neither proves every current and conditionally revealed required field is answered. ApplyPilot must build a deterministic form inventory, re-run it after conditional actions, reject unmapped controls, compare the final browser state with the signed answer plan, and only then expose submit.
answer_provenance: Variables can keep raw secrets out of prompts and caches, but Stagehand does not constrain ordinary answers to ApplyPilot evidence. Resolve every answer before act from profile, resume, approved-answer, or job evidence; attach source IDs/hashes; disallow model-authored facts; and send unknown free text to review.
unsupported_question_fallback: Use observe/extract only to classify the question, then return a durable needs_review state for unknown, sensitive, legal, demographic, compensation, work-authorization, or unsupported free-text prompts. Stagehand can hand control back to Playwright or an owner, but the fallback boundary and no-submit guarantee must be implemented by ApplyPilot.
prompt_injection_resistance: [uncertain] Stagehand recommends variables for secrets and reduced logging, but current primary documentation does not establish a browser-domain allowlist, hostile-page benchmark, or prompt-injection guarantee. Restrict navigation and tools in the surrounding browser/proxy layer, use a minimal system prompt, never expose secrets to page-derived instructions, and test content that attempts data exfiltration or premature submit.
site_permission_and_terms: [uncertain] Stagehand and Browserbase are generic automation tools and provide no ATS-specific authorization. Review the terms and documented interfaces of every ATS/employer, operate only public or user-authorized flows, respect access controls, and do not use managed identity, proxy, or challenge features to bypass restrictions.
irreversible_action_guard: No Stagehand primitive makes submit transactionally safe. Use observe for a preview, but execute final submit only through a separate deterministic function after ApplyPilot checks required-field completeness, provenance, host policy, current lease, dedup, and a one-time approval token. Disable autonomous agent submit during shadow and early canary phases.
concurrency: [uncertain] Browserbase currently advertises 3 concurrent browsers on Free, 25 on Developer, 100 on Startup, and 250+ on Scale; local concurrency is bounded by RAM, model rate limits, browser profile ownership, and site/account constraints. Each ApplyPilot attempt should use its own session/page ownership, and concurrency should be reduced to one for accounts that disallow parallel use.
startup_latency: [uncertain] No official ATS-specific cold/warm timing is published. Cold local mode starts an embedded service and Chrome; Browserbase creates a remote session; both may then wait for DOM settlement and model inference. keepAlive and session reuse reduce browser startup, while cache hits remove inference. Measure these phases separately.
warm_session_reuse: Strong. keepAlive retains local or Browserbase sessions, browserbaseSessionID reconnects to managed sessions, persistent contexts preserve auth, and local/server caches reuse actions. ApplyPilot must add idle TTLs, health checks, profile locks, page reset, and explicit session termination so reuse does not leak one job's state into another.
tracing_and_replay: Local structured logs can include LLM requests/responses, CDP calls/events, and Stagehand operations. Browserbase adds screen recording/replay, network timing, console logs, CPU/RAM, and cost/utilization. Local cache can replay act and agent actions without LLM calls, but replay must be invalidated on page/form changes and prohibited after an ambiguous submit.
deployment_modes: TypeScript or Python locally with Chrome/Chromium, local execution with direct model keys, Browserbase-hosted browser plus Stagehand API, Browserbase browser with API mode disabled, managed Model Gateway, custom/OpenAI-compatible model endpoints, local Ollama, containers, and hybrid BYOB/CDP use.
browser_version_matrix: The documented browser path is Chrome/Chromium over CDP. Stagehand can interoperate with Playwright, Puppeteer, and Patchright Page objects, but this does not establish Firefox, WebKit, WebDriver BiDi, or Safari support. Pin Chrome and Stagehand versions and run the synthetic ATS suite before fleet rollout.
canary_stop_loss: Set per-attempt model, token, action, time, and browser-session budgets outside Stagehand; limit agent max steps, disable submit in shadow mode, and stop on the first duplicate or ambiguous outcome. Suggested promotion gates are equal positive-confirmation quality, lower bootstrap cost per verified apply, no provenance violations, and route-specific failure rate below the control.
benchmark_design: Compare deterministic adapter, current Playwright MCP agent, Stagehand uncached, and Stagehand warm-cache routes. Use repeated synthetic ATS forms for cache invalidation, conditional fields, uploads, hostile text, and crash points; use unique randomized live jobs in ATS strata to avoid duplicate applications. Record cache hit/miss, self-heal, tokens, browser time, fallback, human minutes, and evidence tier.
route_funnel: Track eligible, policy-allowed, Stagehand session started, profile attached, cache lookup, cache hit/miss, form inventory complete, required plan complete, provenance verified, submit token issued, action executed, positive network/DOM confirmation, fallback before submit, human recovery, ambiguous quarantine, and final outcome.
step_context_budget: [uncertain] Initial canary budget: at most 12 model-assisted operations or agent steps, 50,000 input and 5,000 output tokens, five browser minutes, $0.05 candidate-route cash cost before fallback, and no more than one self-heal attempt per action signature. Tune separately by ATS after measuring quality and cache behavior.
cache_replay_yield: [uncertain] Stagehand has the strongest explicit replay proposition in this set: Browserbase server cache and local cache can return/replay actions and agent steps with no LLM inference. ApplyPilot's warm hit rate, invalidation frequency, and safe no-model completion share are unknown; require URL/form-signature versioning and count only positively verified replays.
confidence_intervals: [uncertain] No ApplyPilot confidence interval exists. Use Wilson 95% intervals for verified completion, cache-safe completion, fallback, and ambiguous submit rates; bootstrap cost per verified apply and latency; and compare paired synthetic runs plus stratified live canaries. A 100-job ATS cell near 30% completion has roughly a 9-point half-width, so do not promote from a handful of successes.
historical_bucket_impact: [uncertain] Stagehand could reduce the 44.7% agent/browser-runtime failure bucket through structured actions, self-heal, and cache replay, and reduce repeat model spend on stable forms. It is unlikely to fix the 40.8% preflight/policy bucket or Workday's 1.0% success without routing changes. Replay ApplyPilot history with measured Stagehand cost, fallback, cache, and confirmation rates before claiming savings.
recommendation: CANARY. Test Stagehand as a semantic layer for long-tail forms and as a cacheable bridge from model-assisted discovery to no-model replay. Keep deterministic Ashby/Greenhouse adapters first, keep final submit behind ApplyPilot's guarded function, and compare local Python/BYOB against Browserbase economics. Promote only ATS/form signatures with stable cache validity and positive confirmation.
